title_id: Hadis Kedua: Pelajaran dari Penampilan dan Sikap Jibril dalam Bertanya
content_en: His calling the Prophet (peace be upon him) by his first name only, his walking over the necks of the people and his sitting directly in front of the Prophet (peace be upon him) all added to his appearance of being an apparently ignorant or ill-mannered bedouin Arab. At the end of the hadith, the Prophet (peace be upon him) explained that the bedouin-looking person was the Angel Gabriel who had come to teach them their religion. According to al-Haitami and others, his teaching was both in the form of his questions and also in his behavior. Hence, some conclusions can be made from his appearance and behavior.1 It is not clear to this author whether the commentators noticed that they seem to be making two contradictory suggestions here. First they are stating that the angel came in a very rude fashion and then they are suggesting that his example is one that can be followed. It seems that the key is to distinguish between what the angel did in order to get attention, to make sure that everyone would pay listen to him, from what he did as an example for others to follow. "extremely white clothing and extremely black hair." In other narrations, it also states that his clothing was so clean that there were no traces of dirt upon them. Scholars understand from Gabriel's appearance that it is recommended to have a good appearance and to be clean. 2 This is especially true when one is going to the mosque and when one is attempting to attain knowledge. White clothing is a preferred clothing, especially for the people of knowledge. According to al-Haitami, Umar recommended it for the reciters of the Quran.3 Cleanliness is also an important characteristic of the true Muslim and, like all characteristics of true Muslims, must be even more-so emphasized for the scholars and the students of knowledge. They should set the examples for others. 1 Al-Haitami, p. 59. 2 Al-Haitami, p. 59. 3 Al-Haitami, p. 59. Commentary on the Forty Hadith of al-Nawawi There may be another aspect of note here. Islam places emphasis on both the outward and inward aspects of a human being. Neither aspect is neglected. One can understand from many texts that there is a strong relationship between the two. If one wants to attain knowledge, one must approach that knowledge in the proper manner by first having the correct intention and also by having the proper respect for the knowledge that he seeks to attain. Having proper respect for the knowledge includes being willing to sacrifice one's time and wealth for it as well as outwardly showing respect for it by sitting and appearing in a proper manner while attaining knowledge or passing on knowledge. "There were no signs of travel on him and none of us knew him." He was not from the people of Madinah and yet there were no signs that he had come from travel. Hence, his appearance was something special and immediately attracted the people's attention to him. Perhaps, Allah knows best, this was so the people would watch and listen to what he did very carefully. In this way, they would pay close attention to him, grasp his words and the Prophet's response and then pass it on to others who came afterwards. Umar said that none of them knew the man. This was not simply conjecture on Umar's part. In other narrations, it explicitly states that the people looked at each other and had no idea who this man was.1 "He [came and] sat next to the Prophet (peace be upon him). He supported his knees up against the knees of the Prophet (peace be upon him)" This should be the attitude of those who are truly seeking knowledge. They should try to get close to the teacher so that they can understand and hear everything he says correctly. Unfortunately, one does not always find such an attitude among Muslims today. They have more of a desire to sit in a comfortable way, with their backs against a wall, for example, rather than getting close to the teacher so that they can absorb as much as possible. "and put his hands on his thighs" Al-Nawawi declares that the Angel Gabriel put his own hands on his own thighs. However, in the narrations from ibn Abbas and Abu Amr al­ Ashari, it explicitly states that the Angel put his hands on the thighs of the Prophet (peace be upon him). This was also the conclusion of al-Baghawi, al­ Taimi and al-Teebi. Al-Turabashti also argues that this is the correct interpretation as this is the way a student should sit in front of his teacher. Perhaps it was done so he could best listen to the Prophet (peace be upon him) 1 Ibn Hajr, Fath (Daar al-Fikr), vol. 1, p. 160. 1 Hadith #2: The Hadith of Jibreel and absorb everything the Prophet (peace be upon him) had to say in response to the important questions he was about to pose.
content_id:
Caranya memanggil Nabi hanya dengan nama depannya, berjalan melewati orang-orang, dan duduk langsung di hadapan Nabi semuanya menambah kesan bahwa ia adalah seorang Arab badui yang tampak tidak tahu tata krama. Di akhir hadis, Nabi menjelaskan bahwa orang yang tampak seperti badui itu adalah Malaikat Jibril yang datang untuk mengajarkan agama mereka. Menurut al-Haitami dan ulama lainnya, pengajarannya itu ada dalam bentuk pertanyaan-pertanyaannya dan juga dalam perilakunya. Karena itu, beberapa kesimpulan dapat diambil dari penampilan dan perilakunya.

Adapun pelajaran dari 'pakaian sangat putih dan rambut sangat hitam': dalam riwayat lain disebutkan juga bahwa pakaiannya begitu bersih sehingga tidak ada bekas kotoran padanya. Para ulama memahami dari penampilan Jibril bahwa dianjurkan untuk berpenampilan baik dan bersih, terutama ketika seseorang pergi ke masjid atau ketika sedang berusaha menuntut ilmu. Pakaian putih adalah pakaian yang disukai, terutama bagi para ulama. Menurut al-Haitami, Umar menganjurkannya bagi para penghapal Al-Quran. Kebersihan juga merupakan karakteristik penting seorang Muslim sejati dan, seperti semua karakteristik Muslim sejati, harus lebih ditekankan lagi bagi para ulama dan para penuntut ilmu — mereka harus menjadi teladan bagi orang lain.

Ada aspek lain yang perlu diperhatikan di sini. Islam memberikan penekanan pada aspek lahir maupun batin manusia — tidak ada yang diabaikan. Dari banyak teks dapat dipahami bahwa ada hubungan yang kuat antara keduanya. Jika seseorang ingin menuntut ilmu, ia harus mendekati ilmu itu dengan cara yang benar — pertama dengan memiliki niat yang benar, dan juga dengan memiliki rasa hormat yang tepat terhadap ilmu yang ingin ia capai. Memiliki rasa hormat yang tepat terhadap ilmu mencakup kesediaan untuk mengorbankan waktu dan hartanya, serta menunjukkan rasa hormat secara lahiriah dengan duduk dan berpenampilan dengan cara yang layak ketika menuntut atau menyampaikan ilmu.

'Tidak terlihat tanda-tanda perjalanan padanya dan tidak seorang pun dari kami mengenalnya': Ia bukan dari penduduk Madinah namun tidak ada tanda-tanda bahwa ia baru bepergian. Dengan demikian penampilannya menjadi sesuatu yang istimewa dan segera menarik perhatian orang-orang kepadanya. Mungkin, wallahu a'lam, hal ini agar orang-orang memperhatikan dan mendengarkan apa yang ia lakukan dengan sangat seksama, sehingga mereka dapat memahami kata-katanya, memahami jawaban Nabi, dan kemudian meneruskannya kepada orang-orang yang datang setelah mereka.

'Ia datang dan duduk di samping Nabi, menyandarkan lututnya ke lutut Nabi': Inilah sikap yang seharusnya dimiliki oleh mereka yang benar-benar mencari ilmu. Mereka harus berusaha mendekat kepada guru sehingga dapat memahami dan mendengar dengan benar semua yang disampaikan. Sayangnya, sikap seperti ini tidak selalu ditemukan di kalangan Muslim hari ini — mereka lebih suka duduk dengan nyaman, misalnya menyandarkan punggung ke dinding, daripada mendekat kepada guru agar bisa menyerap sebanyak mungkin.

'Dan meletakkan tangannya di atas pahanya': Al-Nawawi menyatakan bahwa Malaikat Jibril meletakkan tangannya sendiri di atas pahanya sendiri. Namun dalam riwayat dari Ibn Abbas dan Abu Amr al-Asy'ari, secara eksplisit disebutkan bahwa Malaikat meletakkan tangannya di atas paha Nabi. Ini juga merupakan kesimpulan al-Baghawi, al-Taimi, dan al-Tibi. Al-Turabasyti juga berpendapat bahwa inilah interpretasi yang benar, karena beginilah cara seorang murid harus duduk di hadapan gurunya. Mungkin hal itu dilakukan agar ia dapat menyimak Nabi dengan sebaik-baiknya dan menyerap semua yang akan disampaikan Nabi dalam menjawab pertanyaan-pertanyaan penting yang hendak ia ajukan.
content_ar: His calling النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم (peace be upon him) by his first name only, his walking over the necks of the people and his sitting directly in front of النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم (peace be upon him) all added to his appearance of being an apparently ignorant or ill-mannered bedouin Arab. At the end of الحديث النبوي الشريف, النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم (peace be upon him) explained that the bedouin-looking person was the Angel Gabriel who had come to teach them their religion. According to al-Haitami and others, his teaching was both in the form of his questions and also in his behavior. Hence, some conclusions can be made from his appearance and behavior.1 It is not clear to this author whether the commentators noticed that they seem to be making two contradictory suggestions here. First they are stating that the angel came in a very rude fashion and then they are suggesting that his example is one that can be followed. It seems that the key is to distinguish between what the angel did in order to get attention, to make sure that everyone would pay listen to him, from what he did as an example for others to follow. "extremely white clothing and extremely black hair." In other narrations, it also states that his clothing was so clean that there were no traces of dirt upon them. العلماء understand from Gabriel's appearance that it is مستحب to have a good appearance and to be clean. 2 This is especially true when one is going to the المسجد and when one is attempting to attain knowledge. White clothing is a preferred clothing, especially for the people of knowledge. According to al-Haitami, Umar مستحب it for the reciters of القرآن الكريم.3 Cleanliness is also an important characteristic of the true المسلم and, like all characteristics of true المسلمون, must be even more-so emphasized for the العلماء and the students of knowledge. They should set the examples for others. 1 Al-Haitami, p. 59. 2 Al-Haitami, p. 59. 3 Al-Haitami, p. 59. Commentary on the Forty الحديث of النووي There may be another aspect of note here. الإسلام places emphasis on both the outward and inward aspects of a human being. Neither aspect is neglected. One can understand from many texts that there is a strong relationship between the two. If one wants to attain knowledge, one must approach that knowledge in the proper manner by first having the correct intention and also by having the proper respect for the knowledge that he seeks to attain. Having proper respect for the knowledge includes being willing to sacrifice one's time and wealth for it as well as outwardly showing respect for it by sitting and appearing in a proper manner while attaining knowledge or passing on knowledge. "There were no signs of travel on him and none of us knew him." He was not from the people of المدينة المنورة and yet there were no signs that he had come from travel. Hence, his appearance was something special and immediately attracted the people's attention to him. Perhaps, الله knows best, this was so the people would watch and listen to what he did very carefully. In this way, they would pay close attention to him, grasp his words and the Prophet's response and then pass it on to others who came afterwards. Umar said that none of them knew the man. This was not simply conjecture on Umar's part. In other narrations, it explicitly states that the people looked at each other and had no idea who this man was.1 "He [came and] sat next to النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم (peace be upon him). He supported his knees up against the knees of النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم (peace be upon him)" This should be the attitude of those who are truly seeking knowledge. They should try to get close to the teacher so that they can understand and hear everything he says correctly. Unfortunately, one does not always find such an attitude among المسلمون today. They have more of a desire to sit in a comfortable way, with their backs against a wall, for example, rather than getting close to the teacher so that they can absorb as much as possible. "and put his hands on his thighs" النووي declares that the Angel Gabriel put his own hands on his own thighs. However, in the narrations from ابن عباس رضي الله عنهما and Abu Amr al­ Ashari, it explicitly states that the Angel put his hands on the thighs of النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم (peace be upon him). This was also the conclusion of al-Baghawi, al­ Taimi and al-Teebi. Al-Turabashti also argues that this is the correct interpretation as this is the way a student should sit in front of his teacher. Perhaps it was done so he could best listen to النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم (peace be upon him) 1 Ibn Hajr, Fath (Daar al-Fikr), vol. 1, p. 160. 1 الحديث #2: الحديث النبوي الشريف of Jibreel and absorb everything النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم (peace be upon him) had to say in response to the important questions he was about to pose.
